content:
5 min read 3 hours ago -- A Beginner-to-Professional Guide with Real Examples If you’ve ever wondered “When does React create a component? When does it update? When is it removed?” — this article will answer all of those questions in the simplest way possible. Introduction One of the biggest reasons developers struggle with React isn’t JSX or Hooks. It’s understanding what React is doing behind the scenes. When you click a button… When state changes… When data loads… When a page disappears… What exactly happens? That’s where the React Lifecycle comes in. Think of it as the journey every React component goes through from birth until death. Once you understand this concept, Hooks like useEffect, useState, useMemo, and useRef suddenly become much easier. Let’s understand it in the simplest way possible. Press enter or click to view image in full size What is React Lifecycle? Every React component goes through three main stages during its life. Birth ↓ Mounting ↓ Updating ↓ UnmountingJust like humans… • You’re born • You grow • You leave A React component follows exactly the same pattern. Imagine This Real-Life Example Suppose you’re ordering food online. The restaurant receives your order. They prepare it. They update the order status. Finally, the order gets delivered. React components behave similarly. Component Created ↓ Component Appears on Screen ↓ Component Changes ↓ Component RemovedThis entire process is called the React Lifecycle. The Three Lifecycle Phases 1. Mounting (Birth) This is when React creates the component for the first time and displays it on the screen. Think of it as: “Hello World! I now exist.” Example: function App() { return

Hello React

; }The moment this component appears… React says: Mounting...The component is now alive. Real-Life Analogy Imagine buying a new phone. The first time you: • Open the box • Switch it on • Complete setup That’s Mounting. What Happens During Mounting? React does several things. • Creates the component • Creates the Virtual DOM • Renders the JSX • Displays it in the browser If you’re using Hooks… useEffect() with an empty dependency array runs here. Example: useEffect(() => { console.log("Component Mounted"); }, []);Output Component MountedThis runs only once. Why is Mounting Useful? During mounting we usually: • Fetch API data • Load user information • Start timers • Connect WebSockets • Add event listeners Example useEffect(() => { fetchUsers(); }, []);Only once when the page loads. 2. Updating (Growing) Once the component exists… Something changes. Maybe: • State changes • Props change • Context changes React re-renders the component. This phase is called Updating Imagine this counter. const [count, setCount] = useState(0);When you click Count = 0↓Count = 1↓Count = 2↓Count = 3Every click causes Updating...Real-Life Analogy Suppose you’re editing your resume. Every time you: • Add a new skill • Change experience • Update your photo You’re updating your resume. The resume still exists. Only the content changes. Exactly like React. What Happens During Updating? React compares Old Virtual DOM with New Virtual DOM Then it updates only what changed. This is called Reconciliation Example Old

Count: 1

New

Count: 2

React doesn’t rebuild the whole page. It updates only 1 ↓ 2That’s why React is fast. useEffect During Updating Example useEffect(() => { console.log("Count Changed"); }, [count]);Every time count changes... Output Count Changed3. Unmounting (Death) Eventually… A component disappears. React removes it completely. This stage is called Unmounting. Example { show && }Initially show = trueComponent appears. Later show = falseComponent disappears. React says Unmounting...Real-Life Analogy Imagine closing an app on your phone. The app disappears. It no longer uses memory. React does the same. Why Unmounting Matters When a component disappears… We should clean up things like • Timers • Event listeners • API subscriptions • WebSocket connections Otherwise, Memory leaks happen. Example useEffect(() => { const timer = setInterval(() => { console.log("Running..."); },1000); return () => { clearInterval(timer); }; }, []);The function inside return runs during Unmounting. Simple Lifecycle Flow App Starts ↓ Component Created ↓ Mounting ↓ Component Visible ↓ User Clicks Button ↓ Updating ↓ User Navigates Away ↓ Unmounting ↓ Component RemovedVisual Timeline Birth │ ▼ Mount │ ▼ User Interaction │ ▼ State Changes │ ▼ Update │ ▼ Component Removed │ ▼ UnmountLifecycle with Hooks The most common Hook involved is useEffect. Mount useEffect(() => { console.log("Mounted"); }, []);Runs only once. Update useEffect(() => { console.log("Updated"); }, [count]);Runs whenever count changes. Unmount useEffect(() => { return () => { console.log("Unmounted"); }; }, []);Runs when the component disappears. Before React Hooks Older React used Class Components with lifecycle methods. componentDidMount() componentDidUpdate() componentWillUnmount()Nowadays, Functional Components + Hooks are the recommended approach because they’re simpler and more readable. Real Project Examples Fetch API useEffect(() => { fetchProducts(); }, []);Runs only once. Search useEffect(() => { searchProducts(searchText); }, [searchText]);Runs whenever the search text changes. Resize Event useEffect(() => { window.addEventListener("resize", handleResize); return () => { window.removeEventListener("resize", handleResize); }; }, []);Proper cleanup prevents memory leaks. Common Mistakes Beginners Make ❌ Mistake 1 Fetching APIs without dependencies. useEffect(() => { fetchUsers(); });This runs after every render, causing repeated API calls. ✅ Correct useEffect(() => { fetchUsers(); }, []);Runs only once. ❌ Mistake 2 Forgetting cleanup. setInterval(() => { },1000);The timer keeps running even after the component is removed. ✅ Correct return () => clearInterval(timer);❌ Mistake 3 Thinking React reloads the whole page. It doesn’t. React updates only the changed elements using the Virtual DOM and reconciliation. Interview Questions What is the React Lifecycle? The sequence of stages every React component goes through: • Mounting • Updating • Unmounting What is Mounting? The phase where a component is created and added to the DOM. What is Updating? The phase where a component re-renders because of changes in state, props, or context. What is Unmounting? The phase where React removes the component and performs cleanup. Which Hook is used for lifecycle operations? useEffect(). How do you run code only once? useEffect(() => { }, []);How do you clean up resources? return () => { };inside useEffect. Best Practices ✅ Use useEffect for side effects. ✅ Always clean up timers and subscriptions. ✅ Keep dependency arrays accurate. ✅ Avoid unnecessary re-renders. ✅ Understand that React updates only what changes. Quick Cheat Sheet Mount ↓ Component Created ↓ useEffect(() => {}, []) ---------------------------- Update ↓ State or Props Change ↓ useEffect(() => {}, [dependency]) ---------------------------- Unmount ↓ Component Removed ↓ return () => {}Final Thoughts Understanding the React Lifecycle is one of the biggest milestones in becoming a confident React developer. Instead of memorizing Hooks, focus on when a component is born, when it changes, and when it leaves the screen. Once this mental model clicks, concepts like useEffect, API fetching, cleanup functions, timers, event listeners, and performance optimization become much easier to understand. Whether you’re building a simple counter, a dashboard, or a large production application, every React component follows the same journey: Mount → Update → Unmount Master these three stages, and you’ll write cleaner, more efficient, and more reliable React applications. Key Takeaways • Every React component has a lifecycle. • There are three main phases: Mounting, Updating, and Unmounting. • useEffect helps you run code during these phases. • Clean up resources during unmounting to avoid memory leaks. • React updates only what changes, making applications fast and efficient. If this article helped you understand React Lifecycle more clearly, consider giving it a 👏, sharing it with fellow developers, and following me for more beginner-friendly React and JavaScript deep dives. Happy coding! 🚀
